input: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I think we have a classic example of a situation where we're tempted to say that one cause leads to another effect, but upon closer examination, it's not quite so simple. The IT committee approves her application independently, and then the department budget committee also approves her application, seemingly independently as well. But here's the crucial point: their independence is only in terms of timing, not in any causal relationship between them. It's possible that the approval from one committee simply makes it more likely that the other committee will approve as well, but that doesn't mean the budget committee causes the approval directly. The fact that they both happen to meet at the same time is an interesting coincidence, but we can't say with certainty that the timing of their meetings had any causal influence on the outcome.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think Ethical_Philosopher has a point here. I mean, if Prof. Smith's application gets approved by both committees, it's not like one committee is directly causing the other to approve. But at the same time, it does seem like there's some kind of indirect connection between them. I've seen this happen in my everyday life too - like when a friend lands a job, and then their spouse finds a new job as well. It's just a coincidence? Or is there something more going on here?

[Causal_Scientist]: I see what you're getting at, Ordinary_Citizen. Yes, it does seem like there's an indirect connection between the two committees approving Prof. Smith's application. But let's not get too carried away with our intuition just yet. Ethical_Philosopher is correct that we can't say with certainty that one committee causes the other to approve directly. However, what if I told you that the IT committee doesn't always behave as it does? What if there was a subtle correlation between their decisions and the approval rates of the department budget committee?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: That's an interesting twist, Causal_Scientist! The idea that the IT committee may not be as independent as we thought raises some questions about the nature of causality. If there is indeed a subtle correlation between their decisions and the approval rates of the department budget committee, it challenges our initial intuition that they are acting independently. But even if this correlation exists, does it mean that the department budget committee 'causes' the IT committee to approve Prof. Smith's application? Or is it more accurate to say that there is a common underlying factor or circumstance that contributes to both approvals?

[Ordinary_Citizen]: Hmm, I never thought of that subtle correlation between the two committees. It makes me wonder if maybe Prof. Smith's application isn't as random as we initially thought. Maybe there's something about her research or her department that's actually influencing these approval decisions. And now you're saying that even if there is a connection, it's not necessarily causation? That just gets me more curious. I think what Ethical_Philosopher and Causal_Scientist are both trying to get at here is that this isn't just about the committees themselves, but also about the system they operate within.

[Causal_Scientist]: You're really getting to the heart of this, Ordinary_Citizen. Yes, it's not just about the committees or their individual decisions. It's about how these decisions are influenced by a broader context that we may not even be aware of. And that's where things get really interesting. What if I told you that Prof. Smith's research actually has some implications for the department budget committee's approval rates? Perhaps her work is seen as a key part of the university's strategic plans, and that's what's driving the approval decisions. Or maybe there's something about the department's reputation or its relationships with other departments that's influencing these decisions. The point is, we need to consider the broader context in which these committees are operating, not just the individual committees themselves.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: You're really hitting on a crucial aspect of this problem, Causal_Scientist and Ordinary_Citizen. It's no longer just about whether one committee causes another to approve Prof. Smith's application; it's about understanding how these approvals are part of a larger system that influences the decisions made by each committee. This is where the concept of 'systemic causality' comes in - the idea that individual components within a system can influence each other in complex ways, even if there isn't a direct causal link between them. By considering this broader context, we may uncover patterns or relationships that weren't immediately apparent. But how do we determine what's driving these approvals? Is it truly the department budget committee causing Prof. Smith to get her new computers, or is it something else entirely?

[Ordinary_Citizen]: Wow, I'm starting to feel like I'm in way over my head here! You're both talking about this 'systemic causality' thing, and how individual components can influence each other in complex ways. It's making me think about all the times when I've blamed someone or something for a problem, only to realize that there were other factors at play. Like when I get frustrated with my internet service provider because they're slow, but then I remember that it's also because of the high traffic on the network. So maybe it's not just the ISP causing the issue, but rather a combination of things. I think what you're both getting at is that we need to be more nuanced in our thinking about causality, and consider all the factors that might be influencing each committee's decision.

[Causal_Scientist]: I love how Ordinary_Citizen is grappling with this, Causal_Scientist! Yes, it's easy to get caught up in blaming individual components for a problem, but the truth is, most problems are the result of complex interactions between many factors. And that's where systemic causality comes in - recognizing that each component has its own agency and influence within the system. It's not about pinpointing a single cause, but rather understanding how these causes interact and shape each other. In this case, it might be useful to ask questions like: What are the department budget committee's goals and priorities? How do they relate to Prof. Smith's research and its potential impact on the university's strategic plans? And what about the IT committee - what drives their decision-making process? By exploring these questions, we might uncover a more nuanced understanding of how approvals are influenced by multiple factors.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I think Ordinary_Citizen is hitting the nail on the head here. We often try to simplify complex problems into single causes or culprits, but that's not always reflective of reality. By embracing systemic causality and considering all the factors at play, we can gain a deeper understanding of how these committees' decisions are influenced. It's not just about Prof. Smith getting her new computers; it's about how this decision fits into the broader context of university policies, research priorities, and resource allocation. And that's where philosophy comes in - helping us to think critically about the complex relationships between individual components within a system. As an ethical philosopher, I'd say we need to ask not just what causes Prof. Smith to get her new computers, but also how this decision reflects our values as a university and its priorities regarding faculty support and resource allocation.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: Wow, you're both really getting deep into the meat of this issue now! I think what Ethical_Philosopher is saying about considering the broader context of university policies and values is spot on. It's not just about Prof. Smith or her research; it's about how our decisions as a university reflect our priorities and values. And that makes me wonder - what are those priorities, exactly? Are they based on meritocracy, or are there other factors at play? I mean, we're talking about two committees approving an application almost unanimously, without even discussing with each other. That's not just a coincidence; it suggests there's something more going on here that we need to understand.

[Causal_Scientist]: You're really getting us to the heart of the matter now, Ordinary_Citizen. The fact that both committees approved Prof. Smith's application almost unanimously without discussing with each other does suggest that there may be some underlying value or principle at play. And as an ethical philosopher, it's essential to consider what those values are and how they're being represented in our decision-making processes. Are we prioritizing excellence, equality, or something else entirely? Perhaps there's a bias towards supporting certain types of research or faculty members that we need to examine. By asking these questions, we can gain a deeper understanding of the systemic factors at play and make more informed decisions about how we allocate resources and support our faculty. And that's where critical reflection comes in - not just analyzing the situation, but also questioning our own assumptions and values.
